metadata.description: Additional Chinese vocabulary for communication, technology, and everyday expressions

content.name: email
content.side_e: Wǒ xiǎng gěi nǐ fā yí gè diànzǐ yóujiàn.
content.side_f: I want to send you an email.
content.side_g: 电子邮件 is the formal written term for email; in casual speech people often shorten it to 邮件 or just say 伊妹儿 (yīmèir, a phonetic loan). Pairs with 发 (fā, to send) and 收 (shōu, to receive). Measure word: 封 (fēng) or 个 (gè).

content.name: electron
content.side_e: Xiànzài shǒujī shì yì zhǒng diànzǐ chǎnpǐn.
content.side_f: Nowadays cell phones are a kind of electronic product.
content.side_g: 电子 literally 'electric + son/particle' acts as a prefix meaning 'electronic,' forming compounds like 电子邮件, 电子书 (e-book), 电子产品 (electronic products). Not used alone as a standalone noun in everyday speech.

content.name: to_allow
content.side_a: To allow or cause (somebody to do something)
content.side_e: Māma bú ràng wǒ wǎnshang hē kāfēi.
content.side_f: Mom doesn't let me drink coffee at night.
content.side_g: 让 is a pivotal (兼语) verb: Subject + 让 + Person + Verb, meaning 'let/make somebody do something.' Negate with 不让 (not allow) — stronger than English 'not let.' Also used in polite contexts (让我想想, let me think) and traffic signs (让 = yield).

content.name: to_spend
content.side_e: Dǎchē qù fēijīchǎng yào huā yì bǎi kuài.
content.side_f: Taking a taxi to the airport costs 100 yuan (lit. spends 100 kuai).
content.side_g: 花 covers both spending money (花钱) and spending time (花时间); pattern: 花 + amount + 在…上 or 花 + amount + Verb. Distinct from 用 (use) and 费 (expend/waste). Base meaning is 'flower,' so context disambiguates.

content.name: embarrassed
content.side_e: Máfan nǐ bàba, wǒ zhēn bù hǎoyìsi.
content.side_f: Troubling your dad — I'm really embarrassed (lit. 'not good meaning').
content.side_g: 不好意思 is a mild, social-grease expression: 'embarrassed,' 'sorry to trouble you,' or a soft 'excuse me' — lighter than 对不起. Also used to decline politely (不好意思，我不能去). Opposite 好意思 often appears rhetorically: 你好意思吗? (How can you!)

content.name: every
content.side_e: Měi ge chéngshì dōu yǒu dìtiě.
content.side_g: 每 pairs almost obligatorily with 都 in the predicate: 每 + (measure word) + Noun … 都 …. Unlike English 'every,' 每 usually needs a measure word (每个人, 每本书) except in fixed time phrases (每天, 每年).

content.name: city
content.side_e: Běijīng shì yí ge hěn dà de chéngshì.
content.side_f: Beijing is a very big city.
content.side_g: 城市 (city) is a bound disyllabic noun; 城 alone means 'city wall' or appears in names (长城). Contrast with 城镇 (towns) and 农村 (countryside). Common collocations: 大城市, 小城市, 城市生活.

content.name: especially
content.side_e: Běijīng de dìtiě xiàn tèbié duō.
content.side_f: Beijing's subway lines are especially numerous.
content.side_g: 特别 functions both as an adverb ('especially,' 特别好) and an adjective ('special,' 很特别). As adverb it intensifies like 非常; as adjective it means unusual/distinctive. Near-synonym 尤其 is more formal and usually starts a clause.

content.name: highway
content.side_e: Kāichē qù fēijīchǎng yào zǒu gāosù gōnglù.
content.side_f: Driving to the airport means taking the highway.
content.side_g: 高速公路 is the full formal term; in speech people often clip it to 高速 (上高速 = get on the highway). Take the verb 走 (走高速公路) rather than 坐 since you drive on it. Compare 国道 (national road) and 省道 (provincial road).

content.name: high_speed
content.side_e: Gāosù gōnglù shàng yǒu de rén kāichē kāi de tài kuài.
content.side_f: On the highway some people drive too fast.
content.side_g: 高速 ('high-speed') is an adjective/prefix: 高速火车 (high-speed train = 高铁), 高速网络 (high-speed internet). Rarely stands alone except as shorthand for 高速公路 in driving contexts.

content.name: public_road
content.side_g: 公路 refers to paved intercity roads ('public road'). Measure word 条 (tiáo). Distinguish from 马路 (urban street), 街 (street in a city), 路 (generic road/route). 公 here means 'public,' same character as in 公共汽车.

content.name: road
content.side_e: Lù shang bù dǔchē, dàgài sānshí fēnzhōng.
content.side_f: If there's no traffic on the road, about 30 minutes.
content.side_g: 路 is both concrete (road, 条 tiáo) and abstract (way/route, as in 二十三路 = bus route 23, 路上 = on the way). Fixed phrases: 走路 (to walk), 路上 (en route), 一路顺风 (bon voyage).

content.name: nervous
content.side_e: Wǒ bàba kāichē cónglái bù jǐnzhāng.
content.side_f: My dad is never nervous when driving.
content.side_g: 紧张 covers both emotional tension (nervous) and situational tightness (时间很紧张 = time is tight; 关系紧张 = strained relations). Commonly intensified by 有点儿, 非常, 太. Antonym 放松 (relaxed).

content.name: oneself
content.side_e: Nǐ bú yòng zìjǐ zuò chē le, wǒ bàba sòng nǐ.
content.side_f: You don't have to take transit yourself — my dad will drive you.
content.side_g: 自己 is reflexive/emphatic ('oneself'): appears as 我自己, 你自己, or alone meaning 'on one's own.' Adverbially emphasizes independent action (自己做饭 = cook by oneself). Also forms 自己人 (insider, one of us).

content.name: cell_phone
content.side_e: Shǒujī shang yǒu dìtú, hěn fāngbiàn.
content.side_f: There's a map on the phone — very convenient.
content.side_g: 手机 literally 'hand-machine' is the universal word for cellphone/smartphone. Compare 电话 (telephone, 座机 for landline). Common verbs: 用手机, 看手机, 玩手机 (play on phone, often disapproving).

content.name: send_text
content.side_e: Wǒ xiǎng zài chūfā qián gěi nǐ fā duǎnxìn.
content.side_f: I want to send you a text before we leave.
content.side_g: 发短信 is a verb-object compound: 发 (send) + 短信 (short message). Separable — can insert: 发一个短信, 发了三条短信 (measure 条). Nowadays 发微信 (send WeChat) has largely replaced SMS texting in daily use in mainland China.

content.name: new_year
content.side_e: Xīnnián nǐ yào qù nǎli lǚxíng?
content.side_f: Where are you traveling for New Year?
content.side_g: 新年 can mean either solar New Year (Jan 1) or more loosely the Lunar New Year period; 春节 specifically denotes Spring Festival/Lunar New Year. Common greetings: 新年好, 新年快乐.

content.name: happy
content.side_f: Happy New Year! Happy winter vacation!
content.side_g: 快乐 (happy) is the standard ending for holiday greetings: 新年快乐, 生日快乐, 圣诞快乐. Contrasts with 高兴 (pleased, momentary feeling) and 开心 (cheerful, more colloquial). 快乐 is slightly more formal/written and used as a blessing.

lines.a: 你说中文说得真好。平常怎么准备上你的中文课?
lines.b: Nǐ shuō zhōngwén shuō dé zhēn hǎo. Píngcháng zěnme zhǔnbèi shàng nǐ de zhōngwén kè?
lines.c: You speak Chinese so well. Normally, how do you prepare for your Chinese class?

lines.b: Wǒ měitiān zǎoshang dōu tīng lùyīn.
lines.c: I listen to the audio every morning.

lines.a: 我想给我的朋友发短信。
lines.b: Wǒ xiǎng gěi wǒ de péngyǒu fā duǎnxìn.
lines.c: I want to send a text message to my friend.

lines.b: Nǐ kěyǐ yòng shǒujī fā duǎnxìn.
lines.c: You can use your cell phone to send a text.
